title: LeBron’s $300K Maybach Stuns Fans — Inside the Custom Build and What It Reveals About Celebrity Car Power
description: LeBron James didn’t just add another car to his collection—he made a statement about status, customization, and the growing influence of celebrity power in the ultra-luxury automotive world. His newest acquisition, a fully personalized Mercedes-Maybach S 680, reportedly valued around $300,000, isn’t just another high-end sedan. It’s a rolling signature. The build features exclusive MANUFAKTUR design elements and incorporates LeBron’s personal logo across the vehicle, turning what is already one
fullText:
LeBron James didn’t just add another car to his collection—he made a statement about status, customization, and the growing influence of celebrity power in the ultra-luxury automotive world. His newest acquisition, a fully personalized Mercedes-Maybach S 680, reportedly valued around $300,000, isn’t just another high-end sedan. It’s a rolling signature.

The build features exclusive MANUFAKTUR design elements and incorporates LeBron’s personal logo across the vehicle, turning what is already one of the most prestigious luxury cars on the market into something even more individualized. For most buyers, a Maybach is the pinnacle. For LeBron, it’s a canvas.

The Maybach S 680 is already known for pushing the boundaries of comfort and exclusivity. But this version goes further by embedding LeBron’s identity directly into the design. The custom logo spanning the car transforms it from a factory-built luxury vehicle into a personal brand extension.

That level of customization reflects a broader trend among high-profile buyers. At this level, it’s no longer about owning something expensive. It’s about owning something no one else can replicate.

For enthusiasts watching from the outside, it raises a familiar tension. While customization has always been part of car culture, access to this level of personalization is reserved for a very small group of buyers.

LeBron’s collection isn’t limited to ultra-luxury sedans. It spans nearly every corner of automotive enthusiasm, from American muscle to Italian exotics. That diversity is part of what makes his garage stand out.

He owns multiple Ferraris, including an F430 Spider, a 458 Spider, and a 599. The F430 was specifically modified to accommodate his 6-foot-9 frame, highlighting a practical side of customization that even everyday enthusiasts can relate to. Fit matters, especially when performance cars aren’t built for larger drivers.

His Lamborghini Aventador takes a different approach, featuring a unique floral wrap tied to his Nike LeBron XI “Everglades” shoes. It’s a reminder that for some collectors, cars aren’t just transportation or performance machines. They’re part of a broader personal brand.

LeBron’s 25th birthday marked a turning point in his collection. Alongside purchasing a Ferrari 599, he received a Rolls-Royce Phantom as a gift from Shaquille O’Neal. That moment added another layer to his garage, blending personal milestones with automotive milestones.

But even among that lineup, one car stands above the rest in terms of value and exclusivity. The Porsche 918 Spyder, worth around $1 million, represents the peak of hybrid hypercar engineering and remains one of the most sought-after vehicles in the world.

That combination of cars shows a collection built over time, not just through purchases but through career milestones and relationships.

LeBron’s new Maybach isn’t his first personalized luxury sedan. He previously owned a Maybach 57S tied to his time with the Cleveland Cavaliers, complete with custom license plates referencing his Ohio roots.

That consistency shows how personalization plays a central role in how celebrity collectors approach cars. These vehicles become markers of different phases in a career, each one telling a specific story.

For the broader car community, it highlights a growing divide. Customization is still a core part of enthusiast culture, but at the highest levels, it’s evolving into something closer to brand-building than traditional modification.

Despite the current level of luxury, LeBron’s connection to cars began long before Maybachs and hypercars entered the picture. At 18, he received a 2003 Hummer H2 from his mother, who took out a significant loan based on his future potential.

That vehicle was heavily customized, featuring multiple screens, gaming systems, and a high-end sound setup. It also included oversized chrome rims and personalized interior details.

The Hummer became a symbol of both opportunity and controversy at the time, but it ultimately marked the starting point of a collection that would grow alongside his career.

Years later, LeBron’s relationship with the Hummer brand came full circle. He became an ambassador for the GMC Hummer EV and added one to his collection, tying his early automotive story to the modern era of electrification.

That evolution mirrors the broader industry shift. From gas-powered SUVs with flashy modifications to electric vehicles backed by major automakers, the landscape has changed dramatically.

LeBron’s garage reflects that shift in real time, blending old-school builds with modern technology.

It’s easy to look at a $300,000 Maybach and see it as out of reach. But the bigger story isn’t just about cost. It’s about influence.

When high-profile figures like LeBron push deeper into personalization and ultra-luxury builds, it shapes demand across the industry. Automakers respond by offering more bespoke options, more exclusivity, and higher price points.

That trickle-down effect impacts enthusiasts at every level. Features that start as ultra-exclusive eventually find their way into broader markets, but often at the cost of increasing complexity and pricing.

LeBron’s latest addition is another milestone in a collection that mirrors his rise from high school phenom to global icon. But it also highlights a shift in how cars are viewed at the highest levels.

When vehicles become extensions of personal brands rather than purely machines built for driving, it changes the conversation around what car culture represents.

The question now isn’t just how far customization can go. It’s whether the industry will continue catering to exclusivity at the top, or if the passion that built car culture in the first place will remain accessible to the drivers who care about it most.

title: Kevin O'Leary says removing Iran from Strait of Hormuz would be a global 'game changer'
description: 'Shark Tank' star Kevin O'Leary says removing Iranian control of the Strait of Hormuz would be a global game changer, calling the shift 'very bullish' for world markets.
fullText:
"Shark Tank" star Kevin O’Leary said removing Iran's control of the Strait of Hormuz would be a global "game changer" and he addressed the economic impact of the United States' war on Iran.

"I don't want to make light of the war… but I'm thinking ahead 90 days here, and I'm liking what I see," he said Wednesday on "The Story."

The O’Leary Ventures chairman discussed how the Strait of Hormuz’s closure is impacting markets, saying a Strait no longer under Iranian reign would bring more stability.

"Every nine months, the Houthis or some other Iranian proxy would blow up one tank just to destabilize that region and cause the insurance prices to go up," O’Leary said. "We're talking about a world that doesn't have that going on anymore."

Nato Heavyweights Balk At Hormuz Mission As Trump Warns Alliance At Risk

"It's a game changer in the sense that all of a sudden, you have stability in the fastest growing part of the earth. It's very, very bullish for world markets," he added.

O'Leary's comments come as gas prices have surged nationwide, up more than $1 since President Donald Trump launched the war on Iran nearly one month ago.

About 20% of the world’s crude oil and natural gas passes through the Iran-controlled Strait of Hormuz, which remains closed to U.S. vessels.

Jack Keane Calls Out Nato's Weakness As Shipping Crisis Grips Strait Of Hormuz

The national gas average currently sits at $3.98 per gallon for regular, compared to $2.94 before the U.S. struck Iran, per AAA.

O’Leary claimed Americans are willing to suffer in the short-term to reap economic benefits following the war.

"They're very sophisticated… They get the joke," he told Fox News. "They are willing to take a small amount of short-term pain for stability and security of energy in perpetuity."

Retired General Calls For Us Ground Operation To Seize Iranian Island, Cut Off Regime's 'Economic Lifeline'

O'Leary outlined how the drop in gas prices could play out once the Strait of Hormuz reopens.

"The minute Hormuz is open, oil prices will drop to mid-70s, as they were prior to the conflict," he predicted. "The market will adjust immediately. It'll take about two weeks at the pump for it to drop."

O’Leary said the potential reconstructed governance of the Strait of Hormuz to ensure it remains open "forever" would provide worldwide security.

"You've got to think about it as a positive for everybody," he said. "Everybody has skin in the game. It's unique in that respect. No other commodity is needed by everybody all the time."
